title: Build a Spectrometer
description: Construct a simple diffraction-grating spectrometer from household materials, calibrate it, and use it to identify the emission spectra of different light sources.
whyItMatters: Spectroscopy is how we know what stars are made of, how we detect pollutants, and how we study atomic structure. Building a spectrometer from scratch teaches wave optics, atomic physics, and the connection between theory and measurement.

steps.title: Build the spectrometer
steps.description: Use a cardboard tube, a narrow slit (razor blade cuts in aluminium foil), and a diffraction grating (old CD or purchased grating film). Mount them so light enters the slit and is dispersed by the grating.
steps.tips: A 1000 lines/mm grating gives better resolution than a CD (~600 lines/mm).

steps.title: Calibrate with a known source
steps.description: Use a fluorescent light (mercury lines) or LED array to calibrate the angular positions of known wavelengths.
steps.tips: Mark wavelength positions directly on the viewing screen or use a phone camera to photograph the spectrum.

steps.title: Observe different sources
steps.description: View the spectra of incandescent bulbs (continuous), fluorescent lights (line spectrum), LEDs, sodium streetlights, and sunlight.
steps.tips: Sunlight shows Fraunhofer absorption lines — dark lines from elements in the Sun's atmosphere.

steps.title: Identify spectral lines
steps.description: Compare your observed line positions to published spectral data for hydrogen, mercury, sodium, and neon. Identify which elements are present in each source.
steps.tips: The hydrogen Balmer series (visible lines at 656, 486, 434, 410 nm) is the easiest to identify.

steps.title: Analyse and report
steps.description: Calculate the grating equation d*sin(theta) = m*lambda for your observations. Report measured wavelengths with uncertainties and compare to accepted values.

deliverables: A working spectrometer with documented construction
Photographs or sketches of at least 4 different spectra
Identified spectral lines with wavelength measurements
Written comparison of measured vs accepted wavelengths

rubric.description: Spectrometer is functional, well-constructed, and produces visible spectra
Properly calibrated against known wavelengths with uncertainty estimates
Correct application of grating equation, accurate wavelength measurements
At least 3 spectral lines correctly identified with element attribution
